read.image: you'd better come over with a crucifix
read.take: He's already counted his sins and agreed they earn hell. He abandoned his family, did the cocaine, bowed to authority, helped a corrupt world along (per note #1), and the verdict is fair. Then he flinches at it. The same mouth that sentences him to burn won't accept the bill: I don't deserve this, nobody does. When the world comes to collect what's actually owed, he turns the tax demand into a passion play. Come over, but bring a crucifix, because he'd die on the cross before he hands over a cent. The defiance is total and half-ridiculous and he means every word of it. Then the turn lands. Doctor, heal yourself. He stops waiting for anyone to punish him and takes the nails into his own hands. The world never gets its taxes. From now on he breaks his own heart instead.
read.contradiction: He sentences himself to hell and refuses the bill in the same breath, sure he belongs in the fire and sure nobody deserves this.

arc.label: The Self-Sentence
arc.scene: He agrees he should burn, then refuses to let hell have him. The verdict and the appeal come from the same voice. He won't be let off and won't go quietly either.

arc.label: Nail Me Down
arc.scene: The debt comes due and he makes a crucifixion of it. Bring a crucifix if you want me to pay. He'd rather be nailed to the wood than owe the world a thing, and he says it like a man who's tired of being asked.

arc.label: He Takes the Nails
arc.scene: Doctor, heal yourself. He fires the executioner and does the job himself. The world still hasn't gotten its taxes. He'll break his own heart from now on, on his own schedule, by his own hand.

lines.line: You'd better come over with a crucifix
I will break my own heart from now on
